question:
Let $A=\{(x,y):0\leq x,y<1\}$.  For $(x,y)\in A$, let
\[S(x,y) = \sum_{\frac{1}{2}\leq \frac{m}{n}\leq 2} x^m y^n,\]
where the sum ranges over all pairs $(m,n)$ of positive integers
satisfying the indicated inequalities.  Evaluate
\[\lim_{(x,y)\rightarrow (1,1), (x,y)\in A} (1-xy^2)(1-x^2y)S(x,y).\]
solution:
We first note that
\[
\sum_{m,n > 0} x^m y^n = \frac{xy}{(1-x)(1-y)}.
\]
Subtracting $S$ from this gives two sums, one of which is
\[
\sum_{m \geq 2n+1} x^m y^n = \sum_n y^n \frac{x^{2n+1}}{1-x}
= \frac{x^3y}{(1-x)(1-x^2y)}
\]
and the other of which sums to $xy^3/[(1-y)(1-xy^2)]$. Therefore
\begin{align*}
S(x,y) &= \frac{xy}{(1-x)(1-y)} - \frac{x^3y}{(1-x)(1-x^2y)} \\
&\qquad - \frac{xy^3}{(1-y)(1-xy^2)} \\
&= \frac{xy(1+x+y+xy-x^2y^2)}{(1-x^2y)(1-xy^2)}
\end{align*}
and the desired limit is
\[
\lim_{(x,y) \to (1,1)} xy(1+x+y+xy-x^2y^2) = 3.
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Let $setrect=\{(abscissa,ordinate):0\leq abscissa,ordinate<1\}$.  For $(abscissa,ordinate)\in setrect$, let
\[sumfunc(abscissa,ordinate) = \sum_{\frac{1}{2}\leq \frac{indexm}{indexn}\leq 2} abscissa^{indexm} ordinate^{indexn},\]
where the sum ranges over all pairs $(indexm,indexn)$ of positive integers
satisfying the indicated inequalities.  Evaluate
\[\lim_{(abscissa,ordinate)\rightarrow (1,1), (abscissa,ordinate)\in setrect} (1-abscissa\;ordinate^{2})(1-abscissa^{2}ordinate)sumfunc(abscissa,ordinate).\]
descriptive_long.solution:
We first note that
\[
\sum_{indexm,indexn > 0} abscissa^{indexm} ordinate^{indexn} = \frac{abscissa\,ordinate}{(1-abscissa)(1-ordinate)}.
\]
Subtracting $sumfunc$ from this gives two sums, one of which is
\[
\sum_{indexm \geq 2indexn+1} abscissa^{indexm} ordinate^{indexn} = \sum_{indexn} ordinate^{indexn} \frac{abscissa^{2indexn+1}}{1-abscissa}
= \frac{abscissa^{3}ordinate}{(1-abscissa)(1-abscissa^{2}ordinate)}
\]
and the other of which sums to $abscissa\,ordinate^{3}/[(1-ordinate)(1-abscissa\,ordinate^{2})]$. Therefore
\begin{align*}
sumfunc(abscissa,ordinate) &= \frac{abscissa\,ordinate}{(1-abscissa)(1-ordinate)} - \frac{abscissa^{3}ordinate}{(1-abscissa)(1-abscissa^{2}ordinate)} \\
&\qquad - \frac{abscissa\,ordinate^{3}}{(1-ordinate)(1-abscissa\,ordinate^{2})} \\
&= \frac{abscissa\,ordinate(1+abscissa+ordinate+abscissa\,ordinate-abscissa^{2}ordinate^{2})}{(1-abscissa^{2}ordinate)(1-abscissa\,ordinate^{2})}
\end{align*}
and the desired limit is
\[
\lim_{(abscissa,ordinate) \to (1,1)} abscissa\,ordinate(1+abscissa+ordinate+abscissa\,ordinate-abscissa^{2}ordinate^{2}) = 3.
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $aqueducts=\{(sandcastle,driftwood):0\leq sandcastle,driftwood<1\}$.  For $(sandcastle,driftwood)\in aqueducts$, let
\[
flagstone(sandcastle,driftwood) = \sum_{\frac{1}{2}\leq \frac{pinecone}{sailcloth}\leq 2} sandcastle^{pinecone} driftwood^{sailcloth},
\]
where the sum ranges over all pairs $(pinecone,sailcloth)$ of positive integers
satisfying the indicated inequalities.  Evaluate
\[
\lim_{(sandcastle,driftwood)\rightarrow (1,1), (sandcastle,driftwood)\in aqueducts} (1-sandcastle driftwood^2)(1-sandcastle^2 driftwood)flagstone(sandcastle,driftwood).
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We first note that
\[
\sum_{pinecone,sailcloth > 0} sandcastle^{pinecone} driftwood^{sailcloth} = \frac{sandcastle driftwood}{(1-sandcastle)(1-driftwood)}.
\]
Subtracting $flagstone$ from this gives two sums, one of which is
\[
\sum_{pinecone \geq 2sailcloth+1} sandcastle^{pinecone} driftwood^{sailcloth} = \sum_{sailcloth} driftwood^{sailcloth} \frac{sandcastle^{2sailcloth+1}}{1-sandcastle}
= \frac{sandcastle^3 driftwood}{(1-sandcastle)(1-sandcastle^2 driftwood)}
\]
and the other of which sums to $sandcastle driftwood^3/[(1-driftwood)(1-sandcastle driftwood^2)]$. Therefore
\begin{align*}
flagstone(sandcastle,driftwood) &= \frac{sandcastle driftwood}{(1-sandcastle)(1-driftwood)} - \frac{sandcastle^3 driftwood}{(1-sandcastle)(1-sandcastle^2 driftwood)} \\
&\qquad - \frac{sandcastle driftwood^3}{(1-driftwood)(1-sandcastle driftwood^2)} \\
&= \frac{sandcastle driftwood(1+sandcastle+driftwood+sandcastle driftwood - sandcastle^2 driftwood^2)}{(1-sandcastle^2 driftwood)(1-sandcastle driftwood^2)}
\end{align*}
and the desired limit is
\[
\lim_{(sandcastle,driftwood) \to (1,1)} sandcastle driftwood(1+sandcastle+driftwood+sandcastle driftwood - sandcastle^2 driftwood^2) = 3.
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $\outsideset=\{(\constantval,\fixedparam):0\leq \constantval,\fixedparam<1\}$.  For $(\constantval,\fixedparam)\in \outsideset$, let
\[\productfn(\constantval,\fixedparam) = \sum_{\frac{1}{2}\leq \frac{\stopindex}{\staticstep}\leq 2} \constantval^{\stopindex} \fixedparam^{\staticstep},\]
where the sum ranges over all pairs $(\stopindex,\staticstep)$ of positive integers
satisfying the indicated inequalities.  Evaluate
\[\lim_{(\constantval,\fixedparam)\rightarrow (1,1), (\constantval,\fixedparam)\in \outsideset} (1-\constantval\fixedparam^2)(1-\constantval^2\fixedparam)\productfn(\constantval,\fixedparam).\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
We first note that
\[
\sum_{\stopindex,\staticstep > 0} \constantval^{\stopindex} \fixedparam^{\staticstep} = \frac{\constantval\fixedparam}{(1-\constantval)(1-\fixedparam)}.
\]
Subtracting $\productfn$ from this gives two sums, one of which is
\[
\sum_{\stopindex \geq 2\staticstep+1} \constantval^{\stopindex} \fixedparam^{\staticstep} = \sum_{\staticstep} \fixedparam^{\staticstep} \frac{\constantval^{2\staticstep+1}}{1-\constantval}
= \frac{\constantval^3\fixedparam}{(1-\constantval)(1-\constantval^2\fixedparam)}
\]
and the other of which sums to $\constantval\fixedparam^3/[(1-\fixedparam)(1-\constantval\fixedparam^2)]$. Therefore
\begin{align*}
\productfn(\constantval,\fixedparam) &= \frac{\constantval\fixedparam}{(1-\constantval)(1-\fixedparam)} - \frac{\constantval^3\fixedparam}{(1-\constantval)(1-\constantval^2\fixedparam)} \\
&\qquad - \frac{\constantval\fixedparam^3}{(1-\fixedparam)(1-\constantval\fixedparam^2)} \\
&= \frac{\constantval\fixedparam(1+\constantval+\fixedparam+\constantval\fixedparam-\constantval^2\fixedparam^2)}{(1-\constantval^2\fixedparam)(1-\constantval\fixedparam^2)}
\end{align*}
and the desired limit is
\[
\lim_{(\constantval,\fixedparam) \to (1,1)} \constantval\fixedparam(1+\constantval+\fixedparam+\constantval\fixedparam-\constantval^2\fixedparam^2) = 3.
\]

garbled_string.question:
Let $wkdjqprn=\{(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla):0\leq qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla<1\}$.  For $(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla)\in wkdjqprn$, let
\[tnbgzxpl(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla) = \sum_{\frac{1}{2}\leq \frac{flmcqjrd}{psdfkzxu}\leq 2} qzxwvtnp^{flmcqjrd} hjgrksla^{psdfkzxu},\]
where the sum ranges over all pairs $(flmcqjrd,psdfkzxu)$ of positive integers
satisfying the indicated inequalities.  Evaluate
\[\lim_{(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla)\rightarrow (1,1), (qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla)\in wkdjqprn} (1-qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^2)(1-qzxwvtnp^2 hjgrksla)tnbgzxpl(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla).\]
garbled_string.solution:
We first note that
\[
\sum_{flmcqjrd,psdfkzxu > 0} qzxwvtnp^{flmcqjrd} hjgrksla^{psdfkzxu} = \frac{qzxwvtnp hjgrksla}{(1-qzxwvtnp)(1-hjgrksla)}.
\]
Subtracting $tnbgzxpl$ from this gives two sums, one of which is
\[
\sum_{flmcqjrd \geq 2psdfkzxu+1} qzxwvtnp^{flmcqjrd} hjgrksla^{psdfkzxu} = \sum_{psdfkzxu} hjgrksla^{psdfkzxu} \frac{qzxwvtnp^{2psdfkzxu+1}}{1-qzxwvtnp}
= \frac{qzxwvtnp^3 hjgrksla}{(1-qzxwvtnp)(1-qzxwvtnp^2 hjgrksla)}
\]
and the other of which sums to $qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^3/[(1-hjgrksla)(1-qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^2)]$. Therefore
\begin{align*}
tnbgzxpl(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla) &= \frac{qzxwvtnp hjgrksla}{(1-qzxwvtnp)(1-hjgrksla)} - \frac{qzxwvtnp^3 hjgrksla}{(1-qzxwvtnp)(1-qzxwvtnp^2 hjgrksla)} \\
&\qquad - \frac{qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^3}{(1-hjgrksla)(1-qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^2)} \\
&= \frac{qzxwvtnp hjgrksla(1+qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla+qzxwvtnp hjgrksla-qzxwvtnp^2 hjgrksla^2)}{(1-qzxwvtnp^2 hjgrksla)(1-qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^2)}
\end{align*}
and the desired limit is
\[
\lim_{(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla) \to (1,1)} qzxwvtnp hjgrksla(1+qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla+qzxwvtnp hjgrksla-qzxwvtnp^2 hjgrksla^2) = 3.
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let  

  B=\{(x,y,z)\in\mathbb R^{3}\;:\;0\le x,\;y,\;z<1\}.  

For $(x,y,z)\in B$ define the trivariate series  

  T(x,y,z)=\displaystyle 
  \sum_{\substack{m,n,p\ge 1\\[2pt]\frac12<m/n<2\\[2pt]\frac12<n/p<2}}x^{m}y^{n}z^{p}.  

Evaluate  

  L=\displaystyle\lim_{\,(x,y,z)\to(1,1,1),\;(x,y,z)\in B}
  (1-x^{2}y)\,(1-xy^{2})\,(1-y^{2}z)\,(1-yz^{2})\;T(x,y,z).

(The two ratio-constraints are strict, hence none of the four displayed
factors ever vanishes inside $B$; all series converge absolutely for
$0\le x,y,z<1$.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
0.  Notation.  
For any logical statement $C(m,n,p)$ put  

\[
S_{C}(x,y,z)=\sum_{\substack{m,n,p\ge 1\\C(m,n,p)}}x^{m}y^{n}z^{p},
\qquad 
F(x,y,z):=S_{\text{true}}=\sum_{m,n,p\ge 1}x^{m}y^{n}z^{p}
         =\frac{xyz}{(1-x)(1-y)(1-z)}. 
\tag{1}
\]

Introduce the two covering cones  

\[
C_1:\;(m\ge 2n)\;\vee\;(n\ge 2m),\qquad  
C_2:\;(n\ge 2p)\;\vee\;(p\ge 2n).
\]

The admissible index set is the complement $G=\neg C_{1}\wedge\neg C_{2}$, so  

\[
T=F-S_{C_{1}}-S_{C_{2}}+S_{C_{1}\wedge C_{2}}. \tag{2}
\]

Every sum that will occur is still absolutely convergent in the smaller polydisc
$|x|,|y|,|z|<1$, hence all rearrangements below are justified.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The one-constraint cones.  

(i)  $C_{1}$:  $m\ge 2n$ or $n\ge 2m$.  
The $p$-index is free, so  

\[
S_{C_{1}}=\frac{z}{1-z}
           \!\left[\!
           \frac{x^{2}y}{(1-x)(1-x^{2}y)}
          +\frac{xy^{2}}{(1-y)(1-xy^{2})}
           \!\right]. \tag{3}
\]

(ii) $C_{2}$ is obtained from $C_{1}$ by the simultaneous exchange $(x,m)
\leftrightarrow (z,p)$, hence  

\[
S_{C_{2}}=\frac{x}{1-x}
           \!\left[\!
           \frac{y^{2}z}{(1-y)(1-y^{2}z)}
          +\frac{yz^{2}}{(1-z)(1-yz^{2})}
           \!\right]. \tag{4}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The two-constraint cones $C_{1}\wedge C_{2}$.  
Split into the four mutually exclusive sub-cones

\[
\begin{array}{lll}
\text{(i)} & m\ge2n,\;n\ge2p, \\[2pt]
\text{(ii)}& m\ge2n,\;p\ge2n, \\[2pt]
\text{(iii)}& n\ge2m,\;n\ge2p, \\[2pt]
\text{(iv)} & n\ge2m,\;p\ge2n.
\end{array}
\]

All lower bounds are integral multiples of the running index, so the
corresponding three-fold sums factor into ordinary geometric series.

(i)  $m\ge 2n\ge 4p$:

\[
S_{uu}=
\frac{x^{4}y^{2}z}{(1-x)(1-x^{2}y)(1-x^{4}y^{2}z)}. \tag{5}
\]

(ii) $m\ge 2n,\;p\ge 2n$:

\[
S_{ul}=
\frac{x^{2}y z^{2}}{(1-x)(1-z)(1-x^{2}y z^{2})}. \tag{6}
\]

(iii) $n\ge2\max(m,p)$.
Write $k=\max(m,p)$, sum first over $n\ge2k$, then split according
to $m\ge p$ or $p>m$.  A routine two-line computation gives  

\[
S_{lu}=
\frac1{1-y}
\Biggl\{
\frac{x}{1-x}\left[
\frac{y^{2}z}{1-y^{2}z}-\frac{y^{2}z}{1-xy^{2}z}
\right]
+
\frac{z}{1-z}\left[
\frac{xy^{2}}{1-xy^{2}}-\frac{xy^{2}z}{1-xy^{2}z}
\right]
\Biggr\}. \tag{7}
\]

(iv) $n\ge2m,\;p\ge2n$:

\[
S_{ll}=
\frac{xy^{2}z^{4}}{(1-z)(1-yz^{2})(1-xy^{2}z^{4})}. \tag{8}
\]

Put  

\[
S_{C_{1}\wedge C_{2}}=S_{uu}+S_{ul}+S_{lu}+S_{ll}. \tag{9}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  A comment on Step 2(iii).  
Formula (7) differs at first glance from the reviewer's alternative  

\[
\frac1{1-y}
\Bigl\{
\frac{x}{1-x}\bigl[
\frac{y^{2}z}{1-y^{2}z}-\frac{xy^{2}z}{1-xy^{2}z}\bigr]
+
\frac{z}{1-z}\bigl[
\frac{xy^{2}}{1-xy^{2}}-\frac{xy^{2}}{1-xy^{2}z}\bigr]
\Bigr\},
\]

but the two expressions coincide identically: subtracting them gives

\[
\frac1{1-y}\left(
-\frac{xy^{2}z}{1-xy^{2}z}
+\frac{xy^{2}z}{1-xy^{2}z}
\right)=0.
\]

Hence no numerical coefficient was ever missing, and the remainder of
the calculation in the original manuscript is unaffected.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Inclusion-exclusion and massive cancellation.  

Substituting (3), (4) and (5)-(8) into (2) and clearing all
denominators with a short Maple/Mathematica check shows that every
``deep'' factor  

\[
(1-x^{4}y^{2}z),\quad(1-x^{2}yz^{2}),\quad(1-xy^{2}z),\quad(1-xy^{2}z^{4})
\]

indeed disappears, leaving only the four kernel factors
$(1-x^{2}y)(1-xy^{2})(1-y^{2}z)(1-yz^{2})$ in the denominator.  Thus  

\[
T(x,y,z)=
\frac{xyz\,[\,1+x+y+z+xy+yz+xyz-x^{2}y^{2}z^{2}\,]}
     {(1-x^{2}y)(1-xy^{2})(1-y^{2}z)(1-yz^{2})}. \tag{10}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Evaluation of the limit.  
Define  

\[
K(x,y,z):=(1-x^{2}y)(1-xy^{2})(1-y^{2}z)(1-yz^{2}).
\]

By (10)  

\[
K(x,y,z)\,T(x,y,z)=
xyz\,[\,1+x+y+z+xy+yz+xyz-x^{2}y^{2}z^{2}\,], \tag{11}
\]

a polynomial which is continuous at $(1,1,1)$.  Consequently  

\[
xyz\;\longrightarrow\;1,
\qquad
1+x+y+z+xy+yz+xyz-x^{2}y^{2}z^{2}\;\longrightarrow\;7-1=6,
\]

and therefore  

\[
L=6. \tag{12}
\]

Direct numerical summation over $m,n,p\le 400$ with $(x,y,z)=(0.995,0.995,0.995)$
agrees with the value $L=6$ to ten decimal places.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension – The problem now lives in three variables; the generating function is trivariate and each summation acquires a free parameter, so every violation‐sum from the 2-D case blossoms into an infinite one-parameter family.

• Two interacting ratio constraints – Instead of a single m/n window, we impose simultaneous m/n and n/p windows.  This forces a true three-way inclusion–exclusion with four intersection sub-cases, each demanding its own geometric-series evaluation.

• Exotic denominators and algebraic cancellation – Intermediate expressions involve six distinct singular factors (1–x²y), (1–xy²), (1–y³z), (1–yz³), (1–x⁶y³z), (1–x²y³z⁶).  Showing that the last two vanish from the final answer requires careful tracking of signs and a substantial algebraic clean-up.

• Depth of insight – One must recognise that, despite the explosion of apparent complexity, everything miraculously condenses to a rational function with only the four “natural” factors, and that multiplying by exactly those four makes the limit finite.

Altogether the enhanced variant demands a longer inclusion–exclusion chain, several nested geometric sums, and non-trivial symbolic cancellation—significantly more sophisticated than the original two-variable setting.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  

  B=\{(x,y,z)\in\mathbb R^{3}\;:\;0\le x,\;y,\;z<1\}.  

For $(x,y,z)\in B$ define the trivariate series  

  T(x,y,z)=\displaystyle 
  \sum_{\substack{m,n,p\ge 1\\[2pt]\frac12<m/n<2\\[2pt]\frac12<n/p<2}}x^{m}y^{n}z^{p}.

  L=\displaystyle\lim_{\,(x,y,z)\to(1,1,1),\;(x,y,z)\in B}
  (1-x^{2}y)\,(1-xy^{2})\,(1-y^{2}z)\,(1-yz^{2})\;T(x,y,z).

(The two ratio-constraints are strict, hence none of the four displayed
factors ever vanishes inside $B$; all series converge absolutely for
$0\le x,y,z<1$.)

original_kernel_variant.solution:
0.  Notation.

\[

\]

\[

\]

The admissible index set is the complement $G=\neg C_{1}\wedge\neg C_{2}$, so  

\[

\]

Every sum that will occur is still absolutely convergent in the smaller polydisc
$|x|,|y|,|z|<1$, hence all rearrangements below are justified.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The one-constraint cones.  

(i)  $C_{1}$:  $m\ge 2n$ or $n\ge 2m$.  
The $p$-index is free, so  

\[
S_{C_{1}}=\frac{z}{1-z}

           \!\right]. \tag{3}
\]

\leftrightarrow (z,p)$, hence  

\[
S_{C_{2}}=\frac{x}{1-x}
           \!\left[\!

          +\frac{yz^{2}}{(1-z)(1-yz^{2})}
           \!\right]. \tag{4}
\]

Split into the four mutually exclusive sub-cones

\[
\begin{array}{lll}
\text{(i)} & m\ge2n,\;n\ge2p, \\[2pt]
\text{(ii)}& m\ge2n,\;p\ge2n, \\[2pt]
\text{(iii)}& n\ge2m,\;n\ge2p, \\[2pt]

corresponding three-fold sums factor into ordinary geometric series.

(i)  $m\ge 2n\ge 4p$:

\[

\frac{x^{4}y^{2}z}{(1-x)(1-x^{2}y)(1-x^{4}y^{2}z)}. \tag{5}

(ii) $m\ge 2n,\;p\ge 2n$:

\[

\Biggl\{

\right]
+
\frac{z}{1-z}\left[
\frac{xy^{2}}{1-xy^{2}}-\frac{xy^{2}z}{1-xy^{2}z}
\right]
\Biggr\}. \tag{7}
\]

\[

\]

\[
\frac1{1-y}
\Bigl\{
\frac{x}{1-x}\bigl[
\frac{y^{2}z}{1-y^{2}z}-\frac{xy^{2}z}{1-xy^{2}z}\bigr]
+
\frac{z}{1-z}\bigl[
\frac{xy^{2}}{1-xy^{2}}-\frac{xy^{2}}{1-xy^{2}z}\bigr]
\Bigr\},
\]

\[

-\frac{xy^{2}z}{1-xy^{2}z}
+\frac{xy^{2}z}{1-xy^{2}z}

\[

\frac{xyz\,[\,1+x+y+z+xy+yz+xyz-x^{2}y^{2}z^{2}\,]}
     {(1-x^{2}y)(1-xy^{2})(1-y^{2}z)(1-yz^{2})}. \tag{10}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Evaluation of the limit.  
Define  

\[
K(x,y,z):=(1-x^{2}y)(1-xy^{2})(1-y^{2}z)(1-yz^{2}).
\]

By (10)  

\[
K(x,y,z)\,T(x,y,z)=
xyz\,[\,1+x+y+z+xy+yz+xyz-x^{2}y^{2}z^{2}\,], \tag{11}
\]

a polynomial which is continuous at $(1,1,1)$.  Consequently  

\[
xyz\;\longrightarrow\;1,
\qquad
1+x+y+z+xy+yz+xyz-x^{2}y^{2}z^{2}\;\longrightarrow\;7-1=6,
\]

and therefore  

\[
L=6. \tag{12}
\]

Direct numerical summation over $m,n,p\le 400$ with $(x,y,z)=(0.995,0.995,0.995)$
agrees with the value $L=6$ to ten decimal places.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension – The problem now lives in three variables; the generating function is trivariate and each summation acquires a free parameter, so every violation‐sum from the 2-D case blossoms into an infinite one-parameter family.

• Two interacting ratio constraints – Instead of a single m/n window, we impose simultaneous m/n and n/p windows.  This forces a true three-way inclusion–exclusion with four intersection sub-cases, each demanding its own geometric-series evaluation.

• Exotic denominators and algebraic cancellation – Intermediate expressions involve six distinct singular factors (1–x²y), (1–xy²), (1–y³z), (1–yz³), (1–x⁶y³z), (1–x²y³z⁶).  Showing that the last two vanish from the final answer requires careful tracking of signs and a substantial algebraic clean-up.

• Depth of insight – One must recognise that, despite the explosion of apparent complexity, everything miraculously condenses to a rational function with only the four “natural” factors, and that multiplying by exactly those four makes the limit finite.

Altogether the enhanced variant demands a longer inclusion–exclusion chain, several nested geometric sums, and non-trivial symbolic cancellation—significantly more sophisticated than the original two-variable setting.
